coredata.dc:title: Tetraethylenepentamine-derived carbon dots and tetraethylenepentamine co-immobilized silica stationary phase for hydrophilic interaction chromatography
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Tianpei
Sun, Xiaoyu
Chen, Jia
Qiu, Hongdeng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) was used as precursor and reaction medium to prepare tetraethylenepentamine-functionalized carbon dots (TEPACDs), the resultant mixture was subsequently silanized and then grafted on the surface of bare silica. The obtained tetraethylenepentamine-functionalized carbon dots and tetraethylenepentamine co-modified silica stationary phase (Sil-TEPA/CDs) was characterized by multiple ways, such as Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), elemental analysis and transmission electron microscope, which revealed the successful preparation of the mixed stationary phase and higher density of functional groups on co-modified stationary phase than precursor single-modified stationary phase. The synergistic effect of TEPACDs and TEPA was proved by comparing the separation performance of Sil-TEPA/CDs and Sil-TEPA toward amino acids, nucleosides, and nucleobases, which distinctly enhanced the selectivity of Sil-TEPA/CDs. Thus, 12 nucleosides and nucleobases and 11 amino acids was nicely separated on Sil-TEPA/CDs. By study the influences of the changes of mobile phase composition, mobile phase buffer concentration and buffer pH on the retention behaviors of Sil-TEPA and Sil-TEPA/CDs, it was found that both hydrophilic partitioning and adsorption of analytes on Sil-TEPA/CDs were enhanced benefit from the co-existence of TEPA and TEPACDs, which provided the analytes better separation performance. By comparing the column quality of Sil-TEPA/CDs with four commercially available columns, Sil-TEPA/CDs exhibited the best peak asymmetry of 0.98, and second best column efficiency of 43895 m−1 using guanosine as analyte. The RSD (n = 9) of the retention times of five selected analytes on Sil-TEPA/CDs were within 0.30–0.61% during 40 h of continuously elution, which implied excellent stability of prepared packing material.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tetraethylenepentamine
Carbon dots
Hydrophilic liquid chromatography
Stationary phase
Synergistic effect

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chromatographic performance of Sil-TEPA vs Sil-TEPA/CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of TEPACDs (a), Sil-TEPA (b) and Sil-TEPA/CDs (c); overlay of confocal fluorescence and bright-field images of Sil-TEPA/CDs (d) (λex = 405 nm).
FTIR spectra results of Sil-TEPA and Sil-TEPA/CDs.

Separation of 12 nucleosides and nucleobases. Analytes: thymine (1), thymidine (2), uracil (3), adenine (4), adenosine (5), uridine (6), hypoxanthine (7), cytosine (8), inosine (9), guanine (10), cytidine (11), guanosine (12); flow rate = 1.0 mL min−1; T = 35 °C; mobile phase: 85% acetonitrile: 15% ammonium formate (50 mM, pH = 6.2).

Separation of 11 amino acids. Analytes: leucine (1), isoleucine (2), tryptophan (3), methionine (4), valine (5), alanine (6), threonine (7), glycine (8), glutamine (9), asparagine (10), serine (11); flow rate = 1.0 mL min−1; T = 35 °C; mobile phase: 78% acetonitrile: 22% ammonium formate (10 mM, pH = 6.2).

Separation of 12 nucleosides and nucleobases on Sil-TEPA/CDs and four commercial columns. Analytes: thymine (1), thymidine (2), uracil (3), adenine (4), adenosine (5), uridine (6), hypoxanthine (7), cytosine (8), inosine (9), guanine (10), cytidine (11), guanosine (12); Conditions: flow rate = 1.0 mL min−1; T = 35 °C; mobile phase: 85% acetonitrile: 15% ammonium formate (50 mM, pH = 6.2).

Plots of log k vs. water contents in mobile phase (v/v) for 10 nucleosides and nucleobases on Sil- TEPA (a), Sil-TEPA/CDs (b) and 11 amino acids on Sil- TEPA (c), Sil-TEPA/CDs (d). Conditions: flow rate = 1.0 mL min−1, T = 35 °C; mobile phase: 10% ammonium formate aqueous solution (200 mM, pH = 6.2) with varying contents of acetonitrile and water.

Effect of buffer concentration on the retention factor (k) of 10 nucleosides and nucleobases with Sil- TEPA (a), Sil-TEPA/CDs (b); conditions: flow rate = 1.0 mL min−1, T = 35 °C; mobile phase: 85% acetonitrile: 15% ammonium formate (pH = 6.62), Effect of buffer concentration on the retention factor (k) of 11 amino acids with Sil- TEPA (c), Sil-TEPA/CDs (d); conditions: flow rate = 1.0 mL min−1, T = 35 °C; mobile phase: 75% acetonitrile: 25% ammonium formate (pH = 6.62).

Effect of buffer pH on the retention factor (k) of 10 nucleosides and nucleobases with Sil- TEPA (a), Sil-TEPA/CDs (b); conditions: flow rate = 1.0 mL min−1, T = 35 °C; mobile phase: 85% acetonitrile: 15% 100 mM ammonium formate, Effect of buffer pH on the retention factor (k) of 11 amino acids with Sil- TEPA (c), Sil-TEPA/CDs (d); conditions: flow rate = 1.0 mL min−1, T = 35 °C; mobile phase: 75% acetonitrile: 25% 100 mM ammonium formate.
Elemental analysis results of Sil-TEPA and Sil-TEPA/CDs.
BET analysis results of Sil-TEPA and Sil-TEPA/CDs.

head.ce:title: Tetraethylenepentamine-derived carbon dots and tetraethylenepentamine co-immobilized silica stationary phase for hydrophilic interaction chromatography

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng, Jiangsu, 224000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng, Jiangsu, 224000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cUniversity of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Tianpei Cai and Xiaoyu Sun contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) was used as precursor and reaction medium to prepare tetraethylenepentamine-functionalized carbon dots (TEPACDs), the resultant mixture was subsequently silanized and then grafted on the surface of bare silica. The obtained tetraethylenepentamine-functionalized carbon dots and tetraethylenepentamine co-modified silica stationary phase (Sil-TEPA/CDs) was characterized by multiple ways, such as Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), elemental analysis and transmission electron microscope, which revealed the successful preparation of the mixed stationary phase and higher density of functional groups on co-modified stationary phase than precursor single-modified stationary phase. The synergistic effect of TEPACDs and TEPA was proved by comparing the separation performance of Sil-TEPA/CDs and Sil-TEPA toward amino acids, nucleosides, and nucleobases, which distinctly enhanced the selectivity of Sil-TEPA/CDs. Thus, 12 nucleosides and nucleobases and 11 amino acids was nicely separated on Sil-TEPA/CDs. By study the influences of the changes of mobile phase composition, mobile phase buffer concentration and buffer pH on the retention behaviors of Sil-TEPA and Sil-TEPA/CDs, it was found that both hydrophilic partitioning and adsorption of analytes on Sil-TEPA/CDs were enhanced benefit from the co-existence of TEPA and TEPACDs, which provided the analytes better separation performance. By comparing the column quality of Sil-TEPA/CDs with four commercially available columns, Sil-TEPA/CDs exhibited the best peak asymmetry of 0.98, and second best column efficiency of 43895 m−1 using guanosine as analyte. The RSD (n = 9) of the retention times of five selected analytes on Sil-TEPA/CDs were within 0.30–0.61% during 40 h of continuously elution, which implied excellent stability of prepared packing material.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the interdisciplinary studies of nanomaterials (NMs) over the past few decades, chromatographic researchers devoted their effort to explore feasibility of NMs in chromatographic application, and had greatly expanded the diversity of stationary phases [1,2]. Amongst them, carbonaceous nanomaterials (CNMs), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), and metal oxide nanoparticles have attracted great interest due to their special chemical and morphological properties [3–6]. For instance, CNMs such as graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and fullerenes are characterized by their large surface area/volume and giant π-conjugation system, which enable them stronger adsorption capacity than other materials, this advantage make them good stationary phases for the separation of high polar aromatic analytes [5,7,8]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and covalent organic framework (COFs) were also used as chromatographic stationary phase owing to their porosity, uniform pore sizes, and excellent designability in structure [9–15]. One representative example is the homochiral MOFs, which possess single-handed helical channels on the surface, were found to be effective for chiral separation. Besides, metal oxide nanoparticles-based materials (e.g., ZrO2, TiO2) were introduced as alternative support materials for stationary phase due to their high stability under extreme pH and high temperature conditions, which overcame the drawbacks of traditional silica support under such chromatographic conditions [16,17].

Even above-mentioned carbonaceous nanomaterials stationary phases exhibit satisfactory separation performance toward some specific analytes, they are still beset by poor column efficiency due to irregular morphologies and non-uniform distribution on support materials. To avoid these disadvantages, our research group firstly proposed using carbon dots (CDs) as stationary phase for HPLC [3,18,19]. Carbon dots, as a class of zero-dimensional quasi-spherical carbonaceous nanomaterials [20], have many favorable properties that benefit the chromatographic separation efficiency when used as stationary phases, such as rich and tunable surface functional groups, facile synthesis; in addition, the quasi-spherical morphology and nanoscale size (<10 nm) of CDs will not hinder the mass-transfer rate. Up to now, chromatographic performance of CDs-based stationary phases with various surface functional groups have been studied in suitable chromatographic modes [21–24]. The CDs prepared by “bottom-up” organic carbonization approaches usually exhibited hydrophilic property, which are ideal stationary phase material for HILIC, and this has been proved by their good HILIC selectivity reported in many works [25–27].

Based on the impressive performances of CDs stationary phases in HILIC mode, our group further explored the chromatographic behavior of CDs and their precursors co-modified silica stationary phase [18,28]. The experimental results proved the existence of synergistic effect between CDs and their precursors, which remarkably enhanced the hydrophilic selectivity of the as-prepared stationary phase. Thus, a more in-depth study towards this kind of mixed stationary phase would be highly significant, especially for the screening of better CDs species and more effective preparation methods.

The length and skeleton structure of the bonding phase is believed to have significant influence to the of stationary phases in many chromatographic modes. For example, ion chromatographic packing material with branched bonding phase exhibited better separation efficiency as compared with linear structure bonding phase [29]; reverse phase liquid chromatographic stationary phases with extended alkyl chain length provided a higher shape-selectivity [30,31]. As for HILIC stationary phase, Kip et al. found that the zwitterionic ligand with longer chain length exhibited better hydrophilic selectivity than short ones, even zwitterionic ligand with shorter chain length have much higher concentration on support surface [32]. Since hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (PEI) has been used as precursor to prepare CDs and precursors co-modified HILIC stationary phase which showed promising separation performance [18], it would be interesting to study whether linear multi-amines could provide better separation selectivity when used as precursors for the preparation of above-mentioned mixed stationary phase.

In this work, a linear multi-amine named tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) was selected as precursor to synthesize TEPA functionalized carbon dots (TEPACDs) and TEPA co-modified stationary phase (Sil-TEPA/CDs). Because TEPA have relatively high boiling point (340.3 °C) and thermostability, it is feasible to use TEPA as both precursor and reaction solvent to proceed the synthesis of TEPACDs. After removing the extra TEPA, the resulted mixture can be grafted on the silica sphere to prepare mixed stationary phase. The separation performance of the obtained stationary phase Sil-TEPA/CDs in HILIC mode was tested using amino acids, nucleosides, and nucleobases as analytes. The separation mechanism of Sil-TEPA/CDs was also systematically studied by comparing its chromatographic performance with other HILIC stationary phases.

Spherical porous silica (diameter: 5 µm, pore size: 90 Å, surface area: 306 m2 g−1) were obtained from Fuji Silysia Chemical Ltd. (Aichi, Japan,). Tetraethylenepentamine (95%) was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). γ-Isocyanatopropyltriethoxysilane (ICPTES) (98%) and anhydrous citric acid (99%) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The test solutes used for chromatographic evaluation were all supplied by Aladdin Reagent Ltd. (Shanghai, China), including nucleosides (thymidine, adenosine, uridine, inosine, cytidine, guanosine), nucleobases (thymine, uracil, adenine, hypoxanthine, cytosine, guanine) and amino acids (leucine, isoleucine, tryptophan, methionine, valine, alanine, threonine, glycine, glutamine, asparaginase, serine). Acetonitrile (HPLC grade) and ammonium acetate (99%) was from Energy Chemical Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other solvents were analytical grade and used without further purification.

The FTIR spectra were obtained from an IFS 120HR Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The carbon, hydrogen and nitrogen contents of the packing materials were determined by Vario EL III elemental analyzer (Hanau, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging was obtained from a Tecnai G2 TF20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). Laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) imaging were acquired upon a FV1200 Laser Scanning Confocal Microscope (Olympus, Japan). The N2 adsorption surface area measurements were carried out by BET technique using a Micrometritics ASPS 2010 analyzer (USA).

Four commercial HPLC columns were used for separation performance comparison, including XBridge HILIC column, XBridge BEH Amide column (both from Waters Corporation), PRONTOSIL 120–5-DIOL column (Bischoff Chromatography, Germany) and GS-120–5-APS column (Global Chromatography Co., Ltd., China). The dimensions of columns are 4.6 mm i.d. × 150 mm length and particles size of the packing materials are 5 µm. The HPLC tests were run on an Agilent 1260 Infinity Series modular system with quaternary pumps. The detection of nucleosides and nucleobases were realized by diode array detector (Agilent Technologies, USA), UV detection wavelength was 254 nm; an Alltech 3300 evaporative light scattering detector (Grace, USA) with GCK3302 air generator (BCHP Analytical Technology Institute, China) was applied to detect amino acids analytes. The evaporative light scattering detector (ELSD) was set as follows: gas flow, 1.5 L min−1; evaporative temperature, 65 °C; photomultiplier, 1; gas pressure, 0.50 Mpa.

TEPACDs were prepared by pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid in liquidus TEPA according to a referencing method [33]. Briefly, 5.0 g TEPA was degassed with nitrogen for 10 min, and then heated to 240 °C. Then, 1.0 g anhydrous citric acid was quickly added in to the heated TEPA with vigorous stirring. After holding the reaction temperature at 240 °C for 1 min, the redundant TEPA in the resulted reddish-brown mixture was removed by nitrogen blow at 190 °C. The nitrogen-blowing stopped when the weight of the mixture reduced to 2.0 g. Then, the obtained TEPACDs and TEPA mixture was kept for the following experiment.

Firstly, 2.0 g of the TEPACDs and TEPA mixture was suspended in 20 mL anhydrous acetonitrile. Then, a solution containing 2.0 g ICPTES and 10 mL anhydrous acetonitrile was dropwisely added into the former suspension under continuous stirring within 3 h. The reaction temperature was kept at 0 °C with ice-water bath in the first one hour. After the addition of ICPTES solution was finished, the reaction continued for another 11 h in N2 atmosphere. Finally, 2.5 g silica sphere was dispersed into the reactant solution and mechanical stirred for 24 h at 77 °C in N2 atmosphere. The obtained material (Sil-TEPA/CDs) was successively washed with acetonitrile, water, ethanol, and acetone, then dried at 60 °C in vacuum oven. Synthetic procedure was as shown in Fig. 1 .

As comparative experiment, stationary phase with TEPA as the bonding phase (Sil-TEPA) was prepared. By comparing the physical properties of Sil-TEPA/CDs and Sil-TEPA, the modification of TEPACDs on Sil-TEPA/CDs can be better characterized, and by comparing the chromatographic performance of Sil-TEPA and Sil-TEPA/CDs, the advantages of co-immobilization of TEPACDs nanoparticles and TEPA molecules can be better demonstrated. Briefly, 1.51 g (8 mmol) of TEPA was silanized by the reaction with equivalent ICPTES using the same method as above. The modification of silanized TEPA on silica and the following material washing and drying procedures were also identical to the preparation of Sil-TEPA/CDs.

2.2 g of Sil-TEPA and Sil-TEPA/CDs stationary phases were slurry packed into stainless steel columns (150 mm × 4.6 mm i.d.) with 21 mL ultrapure water as slurry solvent (slurry concentration was around 0.1 g/mL) and methanol as propulsion solvent, separately. During the column packing, the packing pressure was controlled at 40 MPa for 20 min, then removed the column from the column packing machine to finish the column packing.

In order to confirm the reproducibility of the preparation of the stationary phase coatings, three batches of Sil-TEPA/CDs were prepared with the identical preparation procedures and column packing conditions (see Fig. S1 for reproducibility results).

Several characterization methods were used to proof the successful preparation of TEPACDs and Sil-TEPA/CDs. The morphology of TEPACDs and Sil-TEPACDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As displayed in Fig. 2a , the diameter of SCDs is about 4 nm. Comparing with the smooth spherical surface of Sil-TEPA (Fig. 2b), the surface of Sil-TEPA/CDs possess numerous granular substances (Fig. 2c). The results demonstrate that a thin layer of TEPACDs have been coated on the silica sphere. Furthermore, laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) was used to characterize the fluorescent property of Sil-TEPA/CDs. Since TEPA and silica matrix are non-fluorescent under the testing wavelength (λex = 405 nm), the Sil-TEPA particles would not exhibit any fluorescence. Besides, due to the fluorescence property of TEPACDs nanoparticles, if a packing material particle was modified with TEPACDs nanoparticles, its LSCM image would exhibit strong fluorescence. The overlay confocal fluorescence and bright-field images of Sil-TEPA/CDs shows that all the silica spheres were covered with homogeneous florescence, which means the modification of TEPACDs on the surface of silica was successfully achieved, and the Sil-TEPA/CDs packing material were homogeneously modified by TEPACDs nanoparticles (Fig. 2d). Elemental analysis results of Sil-TEPA/CDs and Sil-TEPA are displayed in Table 1 , which shows that the C, N and H contents of Sil-TEPA/CDs are higher than that of Sil-TEPA. This fact indicates that the proposed method used for the preparation of CDs and precursors co-modified silica stationary phase can increase the density of functional groups on stationary phase effectively, which could enhance the separation performance of the stationary phase. The FT-IR spectroscopy was adopted to verify the individual or mixed modification of TEPA and TEPACDs on the silica. As shown in Fig. 3 , both Sil-TEPA and Sil-TEPA/CDs share the same adsorption peaks located at 1641 cm−1 and 1567 cm−1, which are ascribed to the stretching vibration of carbonyl and the bending vibration of N–H, separately. The results prove the successful bonding of TEPA or TEPACDs with ICPTES.

The surface areas and pore structures of Sil-TEPA as well as Sil-TEPA/CDs were analyzed by BET measurements. As listed in Table 2 , the BET surface areas, pore volumes and pore diameters of Sil-TEPA are 244 m2 g−1, 0.45 cm−3 g−1 and 5.71 nm, respectively. As for Sil-TEPA/CDs, the BET surface areas, pore volumes and average pore diameters are 184 m2 g−1, 0.29 cm−3 g−1 and 5.11 nm, respectively. The above results shows that the BET surface areas and pore volumes of Sil-TEPA/CDs are much smaller than that of Sil-TEPA, but the difference of pore diameters between Sil-TEPA/CDs and Sil-TEPA is small. The smaller surface areas as well as pore volumes of Sil-TEPA/CDs indicate that TEPACDs not only modified on the surface of silica sphere but also imbedded into the pores of silica spheres. Resultantly, any blockage of the Sil-TEPA/CDs surface pores due to the steric hindrance of TEPACDs would result in reduced pore volume and BET surface area, in contrast with Sil-TEPA, on which the steric hindrance of TEPA molecule can hardly influence pore volume of the packing material. some pores of Sil-TEPA/CDs were blocked by TEPACDs and so the BET surface areas and pore volumes of Sil-TEPA/CDs received a remarkably decrease in contrast with Sil-TEPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chromatographic performance of Sil-TEPA vs Sil-TEPA/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synergistic effect of TEPACDs and its precursor on the chromatographic performance of their co-modified stationary phase can be verified by comparing the separation efficiency of Sil-TEPA and Sil-TEPA/CDs toward a series of analytes. In this experiment, nucleosides and nucleobases as well as amino acids were selected as two kinds of analytes and efforts had been focused on investigating the differences of separation performances with the above two stationary phases. As shown in Fig. 4 , under the same chromatographic conditions, Sil-TEPA/CDs possess better separation efficiency than Sil-TEPA. 12 nucleosides and nucleobases were nicely separated with Sil-TEPA/CDs, while Sil-TEPA cannot accomplish the separation of two pairs of analytes (thymidine, adenine, inosine, and guanine). In addition, Sil-TEPA/CDs also shows stronger retention ability than Sil-TEPA toward all the 12 nucleosides and nucleobases. This phenomenon can be attributed to the much more interaction sites on the surface of Sil-TEPA/CDs than that on Sil-TEPA. That is to say, the synergistic effect of TEPACDs and TEPA enhanced both retention ability and separation performance of the mix-grafted stationary phase Sil-TEPA/CDs toward nucleosides and nucleobases.

As can be seen from Fig. 5 , even Sil-TEPA and Sil-TEPA/CDs exhibit similar retention strength for amino acids, Sil-TEPA/CDs can offer better separation selectivity for the analytes which difficult to separate on Sil-TEPA. Consequently, all 11 amino acids can be separated on Sil-TEPA/CDs, while two pairs of analytes (leucine/tryptophan and asparagine/serine) can only get partial separation on Sil-TEPA. Besides, tryptophan shows enhanced retention on Sil-TEPA/CDs compared to its retention on Sil-TEPA, which may be related to the oriented hydrogen bonding interaction between stationary phase and tryptophan.

In order to better demonstrate the importance of mixed modification strategy for stationary phase preparation, the separation ability of Sil-TEPA/CDs was compared with four commercial columns using 12 nucleosides and nucleobases and 11 amino acids as analytes. Under the same chromatographic conditions, Sil-TEPA/CDs exhibit the strongest retention capacity and separation selectivity, followed by the amino and amide modified columns, the diol functionalized column and the BEH HILIC column exhibit the weakest retention ability (Fig. 6 and Fig. S2). Besides, column quality of Sil-TEPA/CDs was also compared with that of the four commercially available columns. As shown in Fig. S3 and Table S1, the column efficiency of Sil-TEPA/CDs was only lower than amino modified columns, and Sil-TEPA/CDs possessed the best peak symmetry among the tested columns.

Through the comparison of separation performance, we can see that amino-containing stationary phases (Sil-TEPA/CDs, GS-120–5-APS) favors mobile phase with low buffer concentration, under such condition, they exhibit better separation selectivity than the other stationary phases. However, because of higher buffer concentration benefits to the formation of thicker water-rich layer, when electrostatic interaction absented from the separation process, the retention of hydrophilic analytes would get enhanced as buffer concentration increased due to stronger hydrophilic partitioning. This fact is believed to be a general trend in HILIC and had been proved in many works [34–37]. Thus, the neutral stationary phases like diol functionalized and Xbridge HILIC packing materials exhibited the weakest retention for the used hydrophilic analytes under the given chromatographic conditions in which buffer concentration is pretty low (overall concentration of 5 mM). In order to get better separation performance, higher buffer concentration mobile phase is more applicable for diol functionalized and Xbridge HILIC columns, since the separation and retention abilities of such hydroxyl‑containing stationary phases are mainly realized by hydrophilic partitioning effect which rely on the formation of water-rich layer. Besides, higher content of organic solvent in the mobile phase might be needed by these four commercial columns to realize comparable retention capacity of analytes with Sil-TEPA/CDs.

The separation of hydrophilic analytes on HILIC stationary phase usually depends on both partitioning and adsorption mechanisms [38]. The proportion of partitioning and adsorption effects is variable depend on the structures of stationary phases and the nature of analytes. The retention mechanism of stationary phase toward analytes can be confirmed by investigating the effects of mobile phase composition, buffer concentration and buffer pH, which can further verify the synergistic effect of TEPACDs and TEPA on the retention behavior Sil-TEPA/CDs. As shown in Fig. 7 , the retention of 10 nucleosides and nucleobases and 11 amino acids on Sil-TEPA as well as Sil-TEPA/CDs were all getting weaker as the water contents in mobile phase increase, which all follow the typical HILIC mode retention behavior. The difference between Sil-TEPA/CDs and Sil-TEPA is that Sil-TEPA/CDs shows stronger retention ability toward all the analytes compared with Sil-TEPA. It implies that the coexistence of TEPACDs and TEPA increases the number of interaction sites on the surface of Sil-TEPA/CDs and thus enhances its HILIC retention ability. This enhancement effect can boost the retention ability of all polar analytes rather than just work for some certain analytes. Fig. 7c and Fig. 7d show that the retention ability of lysine and arginine decreased more quickly than other amino acids with water content in mobile phase increases. It is because that the retention of amino acids is dominated by both partitioning and adsorption mechanism in which adsorption is rely on electrostatic interaction. In detail, the isoelectric point of lysine and arginine are pIlysine = 9.74 and pIarginine = 10.76, which are greater than other amino acids. Thus, lysine and arginine carried positive charge in the selected mobile phase, and the interaction between the above analytes and amino functionalized stationary phase behaved as electrostatic repulsion. Resultantly, when the water content in mobile phase increases, the water rich layer on the stationary phase would become thinner which leads to weaker partitioning [39]. Moreover, the electrostatic repulsion between the amino functionalized stationary phase and basic amino acids enables them to be eluted earlier [40]. Combining these two aspects, lysine and arginine exhibit stronger tendency to be eluted compared with other amino acids.

The effect of buffer concentrations variation on the retention ability of HILIC stationary phase towards analytes can be mainly attributed to its influence on the hydrophilic partitioning and adsorption strength. Specifically, the water-rich layer would become thicker with the buffer salt concentration increases, which is favorable for hydrophilic partitioning. However, high buffer salt concentration would hinder the penetration of analytes onto the surface of stationary phase, which means buffer concentration increase would suppress the adsorption strength [40]. The retention behaviors of many analytes on HILIC stationary phase can be explained by the competition of hydrophilic partitioning and adsorption.

As can be seen from Fig. 8a , the retention strength for most of the nucleosides and nucleobases on Sil-TEPA presents firstly decreasing and then increasing trends when the buffer concentrations increase from 10 mM to 100 mM, except for cytosine and cytidine which behave as continuously enhancement of retention. However, when the buffer concentration further increases to 200 mM, the retentions of cytosine and cytidine experience stronger upward trend, while the retention trends of other nucleosides and nucleobases encounter an alternation from decrease to increase. This illustrates that hydrophilic partitioning is replacing adsorption to become the main retention mechanism on Sil-TEPA as the buffer concentration increases. Quite different from the property of Sil-TEPA, the retention mechanism on Sil-TEPA/CDs is dominated by adsorption, because all nucleosides and nucleobases (except for uridine, cytosine, and cytidine) show weakening trend as the salt concentration increases (Fig. 8b). The phenomenon implies that higher interaction sites density on Sil-TEPA/CDs remarkably enhances its adsorption effect. As exception, the retention behaviors of cytosine and cytidine on both Sil-TEPA and Sil-TEPA/CDs indicate that their retentions on such type of stationary phases are always dominated by hydrophilic partitioning, while the retention behavior of uridine is speculated to be simultaneously controlled by hydrophilic partitioning and adsorption. Moreover, by comparing the retention factors of all nucleosides and nucleobases on Sil-TEPA and Sil-TEPA/CDs under same buffer concentration, it can be found that Sil-TEPA/CDs exhibit stronger retention toward analytes, which further proves that the coexistence of TEPACDs and TEPA can promote the retention capability of the stationary phase.

As shown in Fig. 8c and d, the retentions of amino acids on both Sil-TEPA/CDs and Sil-TEPA are dominated by adsorption mechanism, in which amino acids (except for lysine and arginine) exhibit weakening retention trends when the buffer concentration increases. Since the interaction between analytes (lysine and arginine) and stationary phase behaves as electrostatic repulsion, the retentions of lysine and arginine should increase remarkably when the water-rich layer becomes thicker, which shield the electrostatic repulsion and enhance the hydrophilic partitioning. In accordance with the above theory, the retention of lysine and arginine on Sil-TEPA/CDs and Sil-TEPA show positive correlation with the buffer concentration. Another notable phenomenon is that, the retentions of lysine and arginine on Sil-TEPA/CDs is weaker than that on Sil-TEPA in mobile phase with identical buffer concentration, while the retentions of the other amino acids on Sil-TEPA/CDs is stronger than that on Sil-TEPA. This result can also be treated as evidence for the existence of synergistic effect which enhance the adsorption strength when TEPACDs and TEPA are mix-grafted on silica packing material.

When the buffer pH value is at 2.3, some of the nucleosides and nucleobases and the amino-containing stationary phase would all carry positive charge, the resultant electrostatic repulsion would hinder the adsorption of analytes on the stationary phase. Since the analytes cannot be retained on the stationary phase by adsorption, the retention of analytes would mainly rely on the partitioning effect. As shown in Fig. 9 a and b, the retention strength of cytosine and cytidine reduce greatly when the buffered pH value decrease from 3.2 to 2.3, which is in accordance with the above conclusion. Additionally, the retention of cytosine and cytidine on Sil-TEPA/CDs still stronger than that on Sil-TEPA even the buffer pH value is 2.3, which can be attributed to the stronger partitioning effect of Sil-TEPA/CDs. This fact indicates that the coexistence of TEPACDs and TEPA can not only enhance the adsorption but also strengthen the hydrophilic partitioning. As shown in Fig. 9c and d, the retention strength of arginine on Sil-TEPA/CDs is weaker than that of Sil-TEPA when the mobile phase buffer pH lower than 3.2. This result can be explained by the stronger electrostatic repulsion between Sil-TEPA/CDs and arginine, which also can be treated as evidence for the existence of synergistic effect between TEPACDs and TEPA. Unlike arginine, the retention of other 10 amino acids on Sil-TEPA/CDs are slightly stronger than that on Sil-TEPA under buffer pH of 2.3, this is probably due to the stronger hydrophilic interaction between analytes and Sil-TEPA/CDs, which offset the influence of electrostatic repulsion.

In order to check the stabilities of the stationary phases, the retention of five compounds including uracil, uridine, cytosine, cytidine, 4-N-butylbenzoic acid were studied for 40 h of continuous elution (Fig. S4). The RSD values (n = 9) of the retention times of the analytes were calculated within 0.30–0.61%. This result indicates that carbon dots and their precursor co-modified stationary phase prepared by the proposed simplified strategy exhibit excellent robustness, which possess similar stability with the stationary phase in former work [18]. Furthermore, the stability of column efficiency of Sil-TEPA/CDs was also tested (Fig. S5), the result showed that the theoretical plate number of Sil-TEPA/CDs ranging within 52,800–57,200 m−1 using cytidine as analyte.

In this work, TEPA and TEPA-derived CDs were co-immobilized on silica successfully via a convenient method, the proposed preparation method greatly simplified the preparation procedure of CDs and their precursor mix-grafted packing material. The co-modified type stationary phase prepared by the proposed method possesses higher density of surface functional groups than precursor single-modified stationary phase, which can provide stronger hydrophilic partitioning and adsorption for the retention and separation of analytes under HILIC mode. Additionally, this study also proved the existence of synergistic effect between TEPACDs and TEPA, which enhanced the HILIC separation selectivity of Sil-TEPA/CDs and benefited to the separation of analytes that difficult to be separated. Regardless of the excellent chromatographic performance, the simplification of the preparation method of CDs and their precursor mix-grafted stationary phase will certainly promote the development of carbon dots modified stationary phase.
All authors agreed with the final version of the manuscript.

Tianpei Cai: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xiaoyu Sun: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Jia Chen: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Hongdeng Qiu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22074154), CAS “light of West China” Program (xbzg-zdsys-202008), Gansu Innovation Group Project (20JR5RA573), and CAS-JSPS Cooperation and Exchange Program (No. E20489GHZ1).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.chroma.2023.464325.
